attributes.stem:
A 42-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a three-week history of fever, night sweats, and fatigue. He has also noticed painful red lumps on his fingers and non-tender red spots on his palms over the past week. He has a history of bicuspid aortic valve but takes no regular medications. He had a a root canal done overseas six weeks ago.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 105/min, blood pressure 125/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 38.5°C. There are tender, erythematous nodules on the pulp of his fingers and non-tender, irregular red macules on his palms. Cardiac examination reveals a harsh systolic murmur at the aortic area. The remainder of the examination is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features highly suggestive of infective endocarditis, including fever, constitutional symptoms, and characteristic skin manifestations in the context of predisposing cardiac disease and recent dental procedure. The painful nodules on the fingers are Osler's nodes, while the painless macules on the palms are Janeway lesions - both though rare, are minor criteria in the Duke classification for diagnosing infective endocarditis. Both presentations can occur in systemic lupus erythematosus, gonococcaemia, haemolytic anaemia or typhoid fever.

Infective endocarditis typically presents with fever, malaise, and various peripheral manifestations. The presence of both Osler's nodes and Janeway lesions is highly specific for endocarditis. The patient's bicuspid aortic valve is a predisposing factor, and the recent dental procedure is a likely source of bacteraemia.

Sweet syndrome (acute febrile neutrophilic dermatosis) can present with tender, erythematous plaques but typically appears on the upper extremities, face, and neck. It does not cause the characteristic distribution of lesions seen in this patient.

Systemic lupus erythematosus can present with fever and skin manifestations but typically causes a malar rash, discoid lesions, or photosensitivity. The acute onset and presence of specific endocarditis-associated lesions make SLE less likely.

Acute rheumatic fever can present with fever and cardiac involvement but typically affects younger patients. The skin manifestations of rheumatic fever (erythema marginatum) are different from those described here.

Meningococcal septicaemia typically presents more acutely with a generalised purpuric rash and signs of severe sepsis. The subacute presentation and characteristic Osler's nodes and Janeway lesions are not features of meningococcal disease.
learningPoints: Osler nodes and Janeway lesions are important peripheral manifestations of infective endocarditis that help distinguish it from other causes of fever.
Osler nodes are painful, raised lesions on fingers and toes, while Janeway lesions are painless, haemorrhagic macules on palms and soles.
The Duke criteria incorporate both major and minor criteria, including peripheral manifestations, for diagnosing infective endocarditis.
references: Gunson T.  Osler nodes and Janeway lesions.  DermNet NZ. 2006.  Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/osler-nodes-and-janeway-lesions
Shimizu T, Tokuda Y. Osler's node. BMJ Case Reports. 2013.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3603816/
